data.title: Illumina forecasts $4.52B-$4.62B 2026 revenue and $5.15-$5.30 EPS while scaling NovaSeq X supply
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Illumina (ILMN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jacob Thaysen opened by saying, "We are off to a great start in 2026 with Q1 revenue, margin and EPS all coming in above our guidance range," adding that performance reflected "strength in our clinical markets and growth across all regions, excluding China" (CEO Jacob Thaysen).
  • Thaysen highlighted "Over 80 NovaSeq X placements in the quarter" and said the company "exited the quarter with a solid backlog, giving us confidence to raise our full year instrument outlook" (CEO Thaysen).
  • Thaysen said Illumina closed "the SomaLogic acquisition with the business performing in line with our expectations on both revenue and profitability" and noted "Clinical made up more than 65% of our sequencing consumables revenue in the quarter" (CEO Thaysen).
  • Thaysen detailed product updates including, "We launched TruPath," and said Illumina remains "on track to launch" its "spatial transcriptomics offering" later this year; he also referenced an "18-month road map" for NovaSeq X (CEO Thaysen).
  • Ankur Dhingra said, "Illumina's revenue of $1.09 billion came in $20 million above the midpoint of our guidance," driven "primarily by better-than-expected instrument sales" and "clinical consumable sales" (CFO Ankur Dhingra).

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year guidance, with Dhingra stating, "We are raising our reported revenue guidance by $20 million... and now expect revenue of $4.52 billion to $4.62 billion" and "now expect non-GAAP diluted EPS of $5.15 to $5.30" (CFO Dhingra).
  • For Q2, Dhingra guided, "reported revenue of $1.12 billion to $1.14 billion, with non-GAAP EPS of $1.20 to $1.25" and said Q2 operating margins are assumed "approximately 22%" (CFO Dhingra).
  • On why organic growth percentages did not move more, Dhingra said, "Decimal points move, hence, the percentage is what it is. There's nothing else more than that" (CFO Dhingra).

Financial Results

  • Dhingra reported Q1 revenue of $1.09 billion, with sequencing consumables revenue of $726 million and sequencing instruments revenue of $118 million; he reported non-GAAP diluted EPS of $1.15 (CFO Dhingra).
  • Dhingra said non-GAAP gross margin was 68.2% and non-GAAP operating margin was 21.9%, noting margin expansion "reflecting increased operating leverage" (CFO Dhingra).
  • On cash and capital allocation, Dhingra reported operating cash flow of $289 million and free cash flow of $251 million, and said Illumina repurchased "2 million shares... for approximately $242 million" and announced "an additional $1.5 billion in share repurchases" (CFO Dhingra).
  • On cost headwinds, Dhingra said gross margin was "partly offset by tariffs" and Q2 assumptions include "near-term inflationary pressures related to freight costs and higher cost of electronic components" with "mitigating actions" expected to show up "from Q3 and into Q4" (CFO Dhingra).

Q&A

  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: asked why the organic growth assumption for the year was not raised more; CEO Jacob Thaysen said, "We are only 1 quarter into the year" and the company is "leaning in by raising both the top line and the bottom line"; CFO Ankur Dhingra said the revenue guide increased by $20 million and "decimal points move" (CEO Thaysen; CFO Dhingra).
  • Puneet Souda, Leerink: asked about clinical vs. research mix in placements and pricing transition; CEO Thaysen said clinical was "more than 60% of the placements" and "it's much more additional incremental placement than necessarily conversion of the 6,000"; CFO Dhingra said on transition pricing, "no change in assumptions" (CEO Thaysen; CFO Dhingra).
  • Tycho Peterson, Jefferies: asked about backlog and whether the roadmap could “freeze” the market; CEO Thaysen said, "Our placements of instruments shows that there's no freezing of any market"; CFO Dhingra said performance obligations are "up more than 20% year-over-year" and Q2 placements "could be close" to Q1 (CEO Thaysen; CFO Dhingra).
  • Madeleine (on for Doug Schenkel), Wolfe: asked about margin progression; CFO Dhingra cited "much higher instrument mix," inflation, and "full quarter of SomaLogic" with mitigation effects visible "from Q3 and into Q4" (CFO Dhingra).
  • Daniel Brennan, TD Cowen: asked about clinical conversion dynamics; CEO Thaysen described "laggards" staying on the 6K due to "regulated assay" considerations and said, "We don't see any deacceleration" in clinical (CEO Thaysen).
  • Patrick Donnelly, Citi: asked about research/applications demand and where SomaLogic revenue sits; CEO Thaysen said there is "alignment from DC around the commitment to NIH funding" but the company "hasn't built much of that into our guide"; CFO Dhingra said SomaLogic is "mostly in the microarrays" (CEO Thaysen; CFO Dhingra).
  • Subhalaxmi Nambi, Guggenheim: asked whether clinical could run above the guide; CEO Thaysen said mid-teens is "a strong growth performance" and "could be upside"; CFO Dhingra said, "we're not seeing any signs of potential deceleration" (CEO Thaysen; CFO Dhingra).
  • Kyle Mikson, Canaccord: asked if guidance reflects new competitive pricing; CEO Thaysen said, "We are very confident in our position from a competitive perspective" and "I don't think that dynamic is changing a lot" (CEO Thaysen).
  • Daniel Arias, Stifel: asked about cost-per-genome comparisons and 35B flow cell pricing; CEO Thaysen said pricing discussions are "based on an elasticity game" and added, "we are not ready at this point to go out and talk about pricing" for 35B (CEO Thaysen).
  • Catherine Ramsey, Baird: asked about low and mid-throughput placements; CEO Thaysen said MiSeq i100 saw "more than 1,000 placements" and mid-throughput remains "more challenging due to the macro environment" (CEO Thaysen).
  • Casey Woodring, JPMorgan: asked about inflation headwind size and placements cadence; CFO Dhingra said on NovaSeq X, "Q2" could be similar to Q1 and "pipeline for the back half also looks fairly robust" (CFO Dhingra).
  • Jack Meehan, Nephron: asked where clinical demand is strongest; CEO Thaysen said, "It goes from oncology, rare disease, into, of course, also NIPT" (CEO Thaysen).
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA: asked whether discounting drove instrument revenue vs. placements; CEO Thaysen said multiunit orders can carry "a volume discount" and emphasized pull-through, referencing "above $1.3 million of pull-through"; CFO Dhingra said some units are "a reagent or a lease kind of model" (CEO Thaysen; CFO Dhingra).
  • Mason Carrico, Stephens: asked about spatial positioning; CEO Thaysen said the category "raises all the boats" and "Illumina is here to play" (CEO Thaysen).
  • Kyle Mikson, Canaccord: asked about X revenue share vs. volume share; CEO Thaysen said, "It's a little bit dangerous to look at this number too precisely" and reiterated expectation that clinical transition reaches "more than 85%" by end of 2026 (CEO Thaysen).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated pressing on guide conservatism, competitive pricing, and NovaSeq X economics (e.g., "Did you update the guidance range to reflect competition launching this summer?" (Kyle Mikson, Canaccord)).
  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and mostly confident in Q&A, repeatedly citing momentum and roadmap execution (e.g., "We do believe that... will continue" (CEO Jacob Thaysen)).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language stayed consistent on clinical strength and research caution, while the current call included more direct discussion of supply constraints and scaling capacity ("we were supply constrained" (CFO Ankur Dhingra)).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Compared with Q4’s initial 2026 outlook, Q1 raised full-year guidance: revenue to $4.52 billion-$4.62 billion (from $4.5 billion-$4.6 billion) and non-GAAP EPS to $5.15-$5.30 (from $5.05-$5.20) (CFO Ankur Dhingra).
  • The instrument stance shifted from Q4’s expectation that placements of "50 to 60 NovaSeq X instruments per quarter" would continue through 2026 to Q1’s commentary that Q1 ran above that range and Q2 placements "could be close" to Q1, with investments to "scale the supply" (CFO Dhingra).
  • The strategic narrative remained centered on clinical momentum and workflow expansion, but Q1 added concrete new-product timing and NovaSeq X roadmap specifics, including TruPath launch and spatial transcriptomics remaining on track for later in 2026 (CEO Jacob Thaysen).

Risks and Concerns

  • Dhingra cited tariffs as an offset to gross margin and outlined inflation pressure from "freight costs" and "higher cost of electronic components," with mitigation expected later in the year (CFO Ankur Dhingra).
  • Management repeatedly described research and applied demand as cautious due to funding uncertainty ("demand remains cautious" (CEO Jacob Thaysen); research consumables "declined 12% ex China" (CFO Dhingra)).
  • Dhingra said NovaSeq X placements faced supply constraints in Q1 ("we were supply constrained") and the company is investing to scale supply (CFO Dhingra).

Final Takeaway

Illumina’s management described Q1 as ahead of its internal plan, driven by clinical demand and higher-than-target NovaSeq X placements, and raised full-year 2026 revenue, operating margin, and EPS guidance while investing to increase NovaSeq X supply. Management framed research and applied demand as still cautious, called out tariffs and near-term inflation pressures, and emphasized continued product expansion (including TruPath, a later-2026 spatial transcriptomics launch, and an 18-month NovaSeq X roadmap) alongside ongoing share repurchases and a newly authorized $1.5 billion buyback program.

data.title: Cable One expects multi-gig capability in most markets by year-end as it targets $2-$5 back-book price reset
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cable One (CABO) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “I’ve now been in the role for a little over 70 days… the work underway across the business is moving in the right direction, but those efforts are not yet showing up consistently in the results,” said CEO James Holanda.

  • Holanda tied Q1 performance to “continued pressure in our more competitive markets, particularly in customer retention,” while highlighting “first quarter connects improved year-over-year” and saying the company is “roughly 2 months into our MSO-wide mobile launch… initial customer response has been encouraging.”

  • On broadband losses, Holanda said, “In the first quarter, we reported 12,600 net residential broadband customer losses on a sequential basis,” adding that “churn was elevated… primarily concentrated within our more competitive markets.”

  • On retention actions, Holanda said the company is implementing “speed upgrades, more gradual stepped promotional roll-offs, AI-driven tools and a new CRM platform expected to go live later this year,” alongside deepening multiproduct relationships including “mobile, Whole-Home WiFi, enhanced online security and comprehensive technical support.”

  • CFO Todd Koetje reported, “Total revenues for the first quarter of 2026 were $353 million versus $380.6 million in the first quarter of 2025,” and said the decrease was “driven primarily by lower residential video and residential data revenues,” including “approximately $10 million” from residential video and “$11.6 million or 5.1%” lower residential data revenue.

  • Koetje said, “Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 of 2026 was $183.3 million or 51.9% of revenues,” and added that “capital expenditures were $68.4 million.”

  • On cash generation and capital allocation, Holanda said, “In the first quarter, we generated approximately $115 million of free cash flow and $500 million over the past 4 quarters,” and reiterated a focus to “execute on our debt reduction.”

Outlook

  • On pricing and ARPU posture, Holanda said, “we continue to expect ARPU trends to remain broadly stable for the year,” while acknowledging “downward pressure from go-to-market initiatives and targeted retention offers.”

  • On back-book pricing over time, Holanda said, “I think overall, in the $2 to $5 range over time” is “realistic and doable,” framing it alongside planned value adds such as “TechAssist… eero’s… security product… [and] mobile.”

  • On network upgrades as a commercial and competitive lever, Holanda said, “Today, approximately 53% of our markets are multi-gig capable, and we expect to expand that capability to most markets by year-end.”

  • On MBI timing and consideration, Koetje said, “there are not adjustments in the purchase consideration… a locked in and disclosed number at $480,” and that the company “did adjust… the anticipated debt that we will assume… into a new range of $895 million to $925 million,” with closing “expected at the beginning of Q4.”

Financial results

  • Koetje said “Operating expenses of $93.9 million” decreased “6%” year-over-year, while “selling, general and administrative expenses totaled $87.2 million or 24.7% of revenues.”

  • Koetje said “Adjusted EBITDA less capital expenditures totaled $114.9 million,” and also noted “During the quarter, we invested $5.1 million of CapEx for new market expansion projects.”

  • On refinancing and debt reduction, Koetje said, “In March, our $575 million convertible notes matured and were repaid in full with a $575 million revolver draw,” and added, “Throughout the quarter, we paid down a total of $90.6 million of debt, of which $86.1 million was voluntary.”

  • On liquidity and leverage, Koetje said, “As of March 31, we had $165.6 million of cash,” “total debt balance was approximately $3.1 billion,” “$700 million of undrawn capacity under our $1.25 billion revolving credit facility,” and “our net leverage ratio… was 4x.”

  • On portfolio actions and one-time impacts, Koetje said, “we completed the sale of certain fiber-to-the-tower contract rights for $42 million in cash,” adding, “We recognized a $26.6 million gain,” and that “the sale reduced first quarter business data revenue by approximately $300,000.”

Q&A

  • Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan: Asked what drove improved connects and whether retention activity diluted ARPU; CEO James Holanda said connects reflected “expansion of the direct sales channel… improvement in the e-commerce channel,” plus “targeted segmented offers… being more aggressive… doing price locks in… 15% of the footprint,” and added ARPU was impacted by “more aggressive go-to-market offers” and “more aggressive on the retention side.”

  • Frank Louthan, Raymond James: Asked about ARPU pressure and whether MBI purchase price could change; CFO Todd Koetje said MBI “net adds were south of 2,000… they lost 2,000,” purchase price is “locked in… at $480,” and assumed/refinanced debt range moved to “$895 million to $925 million.” CEO Holanda later said back-book pricing could adjust “in the $2 to $5 range over time.”

  • Gregory Williams, TD Cowen: Asked about satellite broadband risk and balance sheet next steps; CEO Holanda said satellite “certainly continues to go up” but shows up “in very low circumstances,” and CFO Koetje emphasized ongoing “repurchases at attractive discounts,” while continuing to “actively evaluate longer-term capital solutions.”

  • Brandon Nispel, KeyBanc: Asked how CABO keeps ARPU flat while improving subs and the Q2 net add setup; CEO Holanda said “40% of our footprint, we’re still the only gig provider,” and pointed to “higher speeds” and “value-added services” to manage retention. On MBI leverage, Koetje said close leverage would be “higher than what we previously stated… in and around 4x, but still very manageable.”

  • Samuel McHugh, BNP Paribas: Asked what portion of gross adds come from DSL and EBITDA impact from the tower contract sale; CEO Holanda indicated that in the non-upgraded ILEC areas (about “40% of the footprint”), connects can “over-index slightly,” and CFO Koetje did not give an explicit EBITDA figure, instead referencing “a high single-digit multiple and margins that are slightly higher than what you see from an enterprise side.”

  • Julie Zhu, MoffettNathanson: Asked for an update on fixed wireless equilibrium and structural mix; CEO Holanda said “roughly 80% of our footprint now has one or more FWA competitor,” described Verizon expansion as limited in CABO data, and called T-Mobile/AT&T deployment “slow and steady.” CFO Koetje reiterated a longer-term view of “wired broadband… in more of that 80% area” and “the 20%… wireless… or… satellite.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative to pressing, with repeated probing on ARPU dilution, back-book resets, competitive threats (FWA/satellite), and MBI leverage/debt; questions explicitly challenged sustainability (e.g., “How much of your back book do you think you’re going to need to adjust”).

  • Management: Neutral to slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive/qualifying in Q&A; CEO Holanda repeatedly flagged timing limits (“it remains early,” “too early to draw conclusions”) while still stating confidence in actions (“we believe… can improve retention trends over time”).

  • Compared with Q4 2025: The tone shifted from “foundation… now in place” and mobile “expected in late Q1” to acknowledging that execution changes “are not yet showing up consistently,” alongside more direct discussion of back-book price movement (“$2 to $5 range over time”).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership posture: Q4 featured Todd Koetje as “Interim CEO & CFO,” while Q1 reflected James Holanda as CEO stating he has been in role “a little over 70 days” and emphasizing “consistency of execution across the footprint” as the “biggest opportunity.”

  • Mobile rollout: Q4 described a pilot “in 6 markets” and a broader launch “expected in late Q1,” while Q1 stated the company is “roughly 2 months into our MSO-wide mobile launch,” with “encouraging” early response but “too early” to conclude retention impact.

  • Competitive framing: Q4 characterized FWA as “essentially ubiquitous,” while Q1 quantified overlap (“roughly 80% of our footprint now has one or more FWA competitor”) and added more emphasis on monitoring satellite as “formidable competitors.”

  • MBI financing: Q4 disclosed expected purchase price “approximately $480 million” and net indebtedness range “between $845 million and $895 million,” while Q1 reiterated $480 is “locked in” but updated assumed/refinanced debt range to “$895 million to $925 million.”

Risks and concerns

  • Holanda said “retention remains the primary challenge,” with churn “primarily concentrated within our more competitive markets,” and described mitigation via targeted retention tools, new CRM timing “later this year,” and multiproduct adoption including mobile.

  • Koetje cited business data revenue impacts from the fiber-to-the-tower contract sale and said EchoStar decommissioning reduced revenue by “approximately $50,000 in the quarter” and “roughly $200,000 on an annualized basis,” which he said is “substantially all” remaining exposure.

  • Management described ongoing competitive intensity from FWA, fiber upgrades/overbuilds, and emerging satellite presence, while emphasizing network upgrade progress and continued debt repayment.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a period where operational changes and a newly expanded go-to-market toolkit—mobile, tighter segmentation and retention offers, and added-value services—are underway but “not yet showing up consistently,” while also pointing to $353 million of revenue, $183.3 million of adjusted EBITDA, and $114.9 million of adjusted EBITDA less capital expenditures. Alongside continued debt reduction and liquidity, leadership outlined near-term execution priorities around retention and simplification, targeted a $2 to $5 back-book pricing adjustment “over time,” and reiterated multi-gig expansion from 53% of markets today to “most markets by year-end,” while holding to a locked $480 MBI purchase price with an updated assumed/refinanced debt range of $895 million to $925 million and an expected closing at the beginning of Q4.
